verdict_reasoning: C3 (complement C3) is a secreted plasma protein produced primarily by the liver. It has no transmembrane domain, no GPI anchor, and no other membrane-anchoring mechanism of its own. The mature protein circulates in blood and extracellular fluids as a soluble precursor. Upon complement activation, the cleavage fragment C3b contains a reactive thioester domain that allows it to covalently attach to pathogen surfaces or immune complexes — but this is deposition onto foreign/microbial surfaces (or opsonization of immune complexes), not stable anchoring to the outer leaflet of human cell plasma membranes. Pre-checklist: (1) No cell-surface-directed antibody/ADC/CAR-T targets C3 on the host cell surface — therapeutic anti-C3 programs (e.g., pegcetacoplan) target the soluble circulating pool. (2) Not an ectodomain-shedding TM precursor — C3 has no TM domain at any isoform stage. (3) No covalent/wash-resistant tethering to a host TM partner — C3b thioester covalently deposits onto pathogen/target surfaces but this is opsonization of foreign particles, not stable host-cell surface anchoring (and is immediately regulated/removed on host cells). (4) No documented PM minority pool on human cells — host-cell regulatory proteins (CD46, CD55, CD59) actively prevent C3b retention. (5) No documented cell-death-induced, oncogenic, activation-induced, germline-restricted, or lysosomal-exocytosis ecto-display. No dual localization or cycling pool to the plasma membrane outer leaflet. C3 is a secreted protein with no stable surface-anchoring mechanism on human cells.
